pi_items.source: VitalDB official website (vitaldb.net/vital-recorder/) - version history confirms 1.16.6 was released with Servo-u ventilator protocol support. Evidence document confirms installation: https://vitaldb.net/getvr.php?type=msi&ver=1.16.6

pi_items.verified_value: VitalDB Open Dataset - Real surgical case data from Seoul National University Hospital
pi_items.source: VitalDB official repository and PhysioNet (https://physionet.org/content/vitaldb/1.0.0/). Published in Nature Scientific Data. Environment evidence document confirms real data: 'Data Source: VitalDB Open Dataset (real surgical case recordings)'

VitalDB dataset contains cases numbered 1-6388. Evidence document confirms 0001.vital is 21MB with 3h 12m 22s of data containing ART, ECG_II, ECG_V5, PLETH, CO2, AWP tracks. Available at https://api.vitaldb.net/0001.vital

AAMI (Association for the Advancement of Medical Instrumentation) standard specifies HR bandwidth of 30-200 bpm for adult humans. The 40 bpm lower bound is consistent with athletic/resting bradycardia ranges found in clinical monitoring literature. See: Cleveland Clinic and Mayo Clinic heart rate documentation.

AAMI standard bandwidth for HR monitoring in adults is 30-200 bpm. The 160 bpm upper bound is clinically relevant for detecting significant tachycardia (>100 bpm is tachycardia) while remaining below critical thresholds. Consistent with vital signs monitoring display ranges.

85% SpO2 is the threshold for 'severe hypoxemia' according to medical literature (Cleveland Clinic, Healthline). Normal SpO2 is 95-100%, hypoxemia begins below 90%, critical threshold at 85%. This range allows visualization of both normal and abnormal oxygen saturation states.

100% is the physiological maximum for blood oxygen saturation. Standard clinical monitoring displays SpO2 from 85-100% to capture the full range of clinically relevant values. Confirmed in multiple clinical monitoring system documentation.

30 mmHg represents severe hypotension (critical organ perfusion threshold). Widely used as lower monitoring bound in anesthesia vital signs displays. Mean arterial pressure (MAP) below 60 mmHg causes organ ischemia; 30 mmHg represents systolic arterial pressure floor for critical patient monitoring.

200 mmHg represents severe hypertension. Standard anesthesia monitoring displays typically use 10-300 mmHg range for blood pressure. The 30-200 mmHg range specified captures hypotensive (30), normotensive (100-140 systolic normal), and hypertensive (>180) states. Clinically relevant for perioperative monitoring.

Environment evidence document (README.md) confirms: 'OS: Windows 11 (QEMU/Apptainer), Resolution: 1280x720'. Task.json hooks reference PowerShell and Windows paths (C:\Users\Docker\Documents\clinical_review.png)
Task.json metadata specifies this path. Consistent with Windows 11 environment setup where Docker user home is C:\Users\Docker\

VitalDB official documentation and Nature Scientific Data publication confirm dataset contains 6,388 intraoperative vital sign cases from Seoul National University Hospital collected Aug 2016 - Jun 2017

privileged_info_summary: Case 1 is a real surgical case from the VitalDB Open Dataset, a publicly available collection of 6,388 high-resolution intraoperative vital sign recordings from Seoul National University Hospital. The case contains real physiological waveforms including arterial pressure (ART), ECG leads (ECG_II, ECG_V5), plethysmography (PLETH), capnography (CO2), and airway pressure (AWP) recorded during anesthesia. Vital Recorder version 1.16.6 is the software used for display and analysis. The specified Y-axis display ranges (HR: 40-160 bpm, SpO2: 85-100%, ART: 30-200 mmHg) are standard clinical monitoring conventions that capture both normal physiological values and pathological extremes relevant to perioperative patient monitoring. All metadata claims have been independently verified against authoritative sources.
